# ADR-0002: Coexisting engine + model-driven snip with live-Transcript closure sharing

**Status**: accepted (auto-generated by `ctx-mgmt-pdf-align` review on 2026-05-23)
**Author**: `ctx-mgmt-pdf-align` review session
**Related initiative**: [`initiatives/_archive/2026-05-ctx-mgmt-pdf-align/`](../../initiatives/_archive/2026-05-ctx-mgmt-pdf-align/)

## Context

The `ctx-mgmt-pdf-align` initiative aligned the five-mechanism context pipeline with the documented Claude Code v2.1.88 behavior. The PDF describes `snip` as a *model-driven* `SnipTool` that really deletes messages, whereas the replica's existing snip was *engine-driven* and fold-only. Rather than replace one paradigm with the other, the user explicitly decided to keep the engine version at its current loop position (orphan + ancient-cleared-pair garbage collection, M2) AND add the PDF's model-driven version alongside it (selective deletion the model requests, M4). M3 additionally introduced post-compaction recent-file re-injection, and M4 introduced a token-growth nudge — both of which prepend synthetic messages to the API payload. Each of these forced a concrete cross-cutting decision recorded in HANDOFF Section 2.

## Decision

1. **Two coexisting snip surfaces, not one.** Engine `SnipTool.snip()` (M2) keeps running at `loop.py` `_maybe_snip()` and gained orphan-deletion + ancient-cleared-pair deletion + a `SNIP_BOUNDARY` marker (a new `MessageType` abstraction). The new model-driven `snip_history` tool (M4, `snip_tool_model.py`) is a separate registered tool the model invokes by passing `message_uuids`. The two never contend: engine snip is deterministic GC; model snip is selective intent.

2. **uuid visibility via in-content wrapping, not metadata.** Because OpenAI Chat Completions strips arbitrary per-message metadata fields, `context._normalize_messages()` wraps every `tool_result` block's content in `<msg uuid="...">...</msg>`, gated on `msg.type == TOOL_RESULT` so M3's `ATTACHMENT` user-role messages stay unwrapped.

3. **Live-Transcript closure sharing.** `register_snip_history_tool(registry, transcript)` captures the live `Transcript` by closure. For the model's snips to mutate the history the `AgentLoop` actually reads, the registry and the loop must share one `Transcript` instance. This required moving Transcript construction *before* `build_default_registry(...)` at three call sites (`cli._run_demo`, `cli._build_repl_loop`, `openai_cli._run_task`) — a divergence touching >2 source files, justified as a closure-ownership fix rather than scope creep.

4. **Synthetic-message ordering pinned.** When both recent-file attachments (M3) and a snip nudge (M4) are present, the front-to-back API payload order is `[*attachments, nudge, *kept]`, both injected after budget trimming so neither is ever popped. Pinned by `tests/test_context.py::test_build_nudge_follows_attachments_precedes_kept`.

## Consequences

- **Easier**: The model can now reclaim context space deliberately while the engine still guarantees API-validity GC; the two mechanisms are independently testable (`evaluate_snip_request` is a pure function with no `AgentLoop`).
- **Easier**: recent-file content survives a compaction without forcing the model to re-read (M3 snapshot-then-reattach).
- **Harder**: any new call site that builds a registry + an `AgentLoop` MUST share the same `Transcript`, or model snips silently mutate nothing — an invisible failure with no test against a real endpoint today.
- **Harder / follow-up**: the prepended attachment + nudge dicts bypass the same-role merge in `_normalize_messages` (they are added afterward), so a user-role first-kept message can yield adjacent user-role API messages. Not exercised against a live provider — see REVIEW.md detail findings.
- **Follow-up**: a live OpenAI smoke run is still needed to confirm the `<msg uuid="...">` wrap survives serialization and nests correctly outside `<persisted-output>` for externalized results.

## Alternatives considered

- **Replace engine snip with the model-driven tool entirely** — rejected by the user; the engine version provides deterministic orphan/ancient-pair GC the model cannot be relied on to perform.
- **Carry uuids in a sibling `metadata` field** — rejected because OpenAI Chat Completions strips arbitrary per-message keys; in-content wrapping is the only portable surface.
- **Have the model re-read files after compaction** instead of snapshot-then-reattach (M3) — rejected by the user; re-reading wastes a turn and may hit a changed file.

## Source

This ADR was auto-generated because **a single divergence (the M4 closure-ownership Transcript-wiring fix) touches >2 source files, and HANDOFF Section 2 collectively records ≥2 architectural divergences (`SNIP_BOUNDARY`, `FileSnapshot`/`ATTACHMENT`, `snip_history`/`SnipNudge`)**. The full divergence record is in `initiatives/_archive/2026-05-ctx-mgmt-pdf-align/HANDOFF.md` Section 2's per-milestone "design decisions (deviations from PLAN)" subsections, items: M4 "cli.py / openai_cli.py wiring change is the §7 closure-ownership fix", M4 "nudge sits AFTER attachments, BEFORE kept", M2 `SNIP_BOUNDARY` new abstraction, M3 `ATTACHMENT` filter-in.

Code reference (changes that this ADR explains):
```
git log --follow 8f1d98f..HEAD -- src/simple_coding_agent/snip_tool_model.py src/simple_coding_agent/context.py src/simple_coding_agent/loop.py src/simple_coding_agent/cli.py src/simple_coding_agent/openai_cli.py src/simple_coding_agent/tool_registry_factory.py
```

## Future updates

If a later initiative supersedes this decision:

- **Creating the new ADR is Tier B** (auto-applied by the review session
  when the supersedes trigger fires): write
  `ADR-0003-<reversal-slug>.md` with `**Status**: supersedes
  ADR-0002` and link back to this one.
- **Editing this file's Status line to `superseded by ADR-0003`
  is Tier C** (propose-only — review session writes the suggested diff
  in REVIEW.md "Proposed edits"; a human applies it after confirming
  the supersedes reasoning).

This split avoids the review session silently rewriting historical ADR
status lines without explicit human review.
